summary.overall_regression: Critical regression: health score 10 → 30 (-20 pts). 1 critical incident(s).
summary.headline: DB Time rose 101% driven by: 10 new application SQL(s) (top: 5qzq7mrgbbrn5, 3219s).
headline_evidence: Both periods are I/O-bound (bottleneck type unchanged)
2 SQL(s) regressed without plan change (+5037s cumulative)

comparisons.root_cause_hint: Server process cannot find a free buffer in the buffer cache. DBWR is posted to write dirty buffers to disk but cannot keep up. Causes cascading contention — other sessions also queue for free buffers.
comparisons.pathology_meaning: Server process cannot find a free buffer in the buffer cache. DBWR is posted to write dirty buffers to disk but cannot keep up. Causes cascading contention — other sessions also queue for free buffers.
pathology_investigate: DBWR I/O throughput (db file parallel write avg wait)
heavy DML workload generating dirty buffers

comparisons.root_cause_hint: Single-block read — index lookup or rowid fetch. High count = many index scans. High avg latency = storage problem.
comparisons.pathology_meaning: Single-block read — index lookup or rowid fetch. High count = many index scans. High avg latency = storage problem.
pathology_investigate: missing indexes causing unselective index scans

comparisons.root_cause_hint: Format Block enqueue contention — sessions waiting to format new blocks in the buffer cache. Related to free buffer pressure: when DBWR can't free buffers fast enough, sessions contend on this enqueue while trying to allocate buffer space.
comparisons.pathology_meaning: Format Block enqueue contention — sessions waiting to format new blocks in the buffer cache. Related to free buffer pressure: when DBWR can't free buffers fast enough, sessions contend on this enqueue while trying to allocate buffer space.

comparisons.root_cause_hint: Buffer being read or modified by another session — hot block contention.
comparisons.pathology_meaning: Buffer being read or modified by another session — hot block contention.
pathology_investigate: top segments by buffer busy waits

comparisons.root_cause_hint: Time on CPU — actual execution work, not a wait. High CPU can indicate inefficient SQL or increased workload.
comparisons.pathology_meaning: Time on CPU — actual execution work, not a wait. High CPU can indicate inefficient SQL or increased workload.

comparisons.root_cause_hint: Generic TX enqueue contention — encompasses row lock and index split paths.
comparisons.pathology_meaning: Generic TX enqueue contention — encompasses row lock and index split paths.

comparisons.root_cause_hint: Hot block contention — multiple sessions reading/writing the same buffer simultaneously.
comparisons.pathology_meaning: Hot block contention — multiple sessions reading/writing the same buffer simultaneously.

comparisons.root_cause_hint: Parallel query or serial large table scan bypassing buffer cache. Common with parallel_degree_policy=AUTO.
comparisons.pathology_meaning: Parallel query or serial large table scan bypassing buffer cache. Common with parallel_degree_policy=AUTO.
pathology_investigate: large table scans in top SQL

comparisons.root_cause_hint: Foreground session waiting for LGWR to flush redo to disk on COMMIT. >20ms avg = slow storage. High count = too many commits.
comparisons.pathology_meaning: Foreground session waiting for LGWR to flush redo to disk on COMMIT. >20ms avg = slow storage. High count = too many commits.

comparisons.root_cause_hint: Redo allocation latch contention — high commit rate or large redo generation causing sessions to queue for redo allocation.
comparisons.pathology_meaning: Redo allocation latch contention — high commit rate or large redo generation causing sessions to queue for redo allocation.

new_bottlenecks.root_cause_hint: Foreground session waiting for LGWR to flush redo to disk on COMMIT. >20ms avg = slow storage. High count = too many commits.
new_bottlenecks.pathology_meaning: Foreground session waiting for LGWR to flush redo to disk on COMMIT. >20ms avg = slow storage. High count = too many commits.

improving.root_cause_hint: Redo allocation latch contention — high commit rate or large redo generation causing sessions to queue for redo allocation.
improving.pathology_meaning: Redo allocation latch contention — high commit rate or large redo generation causing sessions to queue for redo allocation.

pkg_log.info('Records inserted from UDT_INHOUSE_FCST to fcstdraftnarrow: '||SQL%ROWCOUNT); COMMIT; MERGE INTO scpomgr.fcstdraftnarrow f USING (SELECT /*+ PARALLEL(8) */ dmdunit, dmdgroup, loc, startdate, dur, type, nvl(TRIM(fcstid), 'OVERRIDE360') fcstid, qty, model from scpomgr.udt_by_fcst ab WHERE record_updated='Y' and exists (select 1 from SCPOMGR.DFU abc where ab.dmdunit=abc.dmdunit and ab.dmdgroup=abc.dmdgroup and ab.loc=abc.loc and ab.model=abc.model)) s ON(f.dmdunit = s.dmdunit AND f.loc = s.loc AND f.dmdgroup = s.dmdgroup AND f.startDate=s.startDate AND f.type=s.type AND f.fcstid=s.fcstid AND f.model=s.model) WHEN NOT MATCHED THEN INSERT(f.dmdunit, f.dmdgroup, f.loc, f.startdate, f.dur, f.type, f.fcstid, f.qty, f.model ) VALUES( s.dmdunit, s.dmdgroup, s.loc, s.startdate, s.dur, s.type, s.fcstid, s.qty, s.model ); all_metrics.label: DB Time/sec
all_metrics.explanation: Total database work per second. Core workload intensity metric.

all_metrics.label: DB CPU/sec
all_metrics.explanation: CPU consumed by database per second. Increasing means more compute demand.

all_metrics.label: Logical Reads/sec
all_metrics.explanation: Buffer gets per second. High values indicate intensive SQL scanning.

all_metrics.label: Physical Reads/sec
all_metrics.explanation: Disk read blocks per second. High value with low Read IO Requests/s = large multi-block full-table scans (direct-path, invisible to Segments section). High value with proportionally high Read IO Requests/s = many single-block index reads. Compare both metrics together.

all_metrics.label: Physical Writes/sec
all_metrics.explanation: Disk writes per second. Increase may indicate more DML or redo/undo activity.

all_metrics.label: Redo Size/sec
all_metrics.explanation: Bytes of redo generated per second. Higher means more DML workload.

all_metrics.label: Hard Parses/sec
all_metrics.explanation: Hard parses per second. >100/s indicates literal SQL or cursor invalidation.

all_metrics.label: Executes/sec
all_metrics.explanation: SQL executions per second. Application throughput indicator.

all_metrics.label: Transactions/sec
all_metrics.explanation: User commits+rollbacks per second. Primary business throughput KPI.

all_metrics.label: Logons/sec
all_metrics.explanation: New session creations per second. Spike signals connection pool churn.

all_metrics.label: User Calls/sec
all_metrics.explanation: User-originated calls per second. Overall application request rate.

all_metrics.label: Block Changes/sec
all_metrics.explanation: Buffer blocks modified per second. Correlates with redo and DML volume.

all_metrics.label: Buffer Cache Hit %
all_metrics.explanation: Data found in RAM vs disk. <95% = excessive physical I/O.

all_metrics.label: Library Cache Hit %
all_metrics.explanation: SQL look-ups that found the cursor in memory. Low = shared pool too small.

all_metrics.label: Soft Parse %
all_metrics.explanation: SQL executions that reused a cached plan. <95% = hard parse storm.

all_metrics.label: Execute to Parse %
all_metrics.explanation: % executes reusing open cursor. Low = cursor caching not enabled.

all_metrics.label: Latch Hit %
all_metrics.explanation: Internal Oracle lock efficiency. <99% = contention in shared memory.

all_metrics.label: DB Time (min)
all_metrics.explanation: Total database active time. Core load indicator.

all_metrics.label: Avg Active Sessions
all_metrics.explanation: Sessions actively working. >CPU count = saturation.

all_metrics.label: Transactions/sec
all_metrics.explanation: Business throughput KPI (commits+rollbacks per second).

all_metrics.label: Elapsed Window
all_metrics.explanation: Observation window length. Large difference = unequal comparison.

all_metrics.label: log file sync
all_metrics.explanation: Foreground session waiting for LGWR to flush redo to disk on COMMIT. >20ms avg = slow storage. High count = too many commits.

recommendations.finding: CPU saturation detected: AAS=294.7 exceeds CPU count 36 x 1.5.
recommendations.action: Identify top CPU SQL, reduce parallelism, or scale CPU
recommendations.oracle_fix:
-- Identify top CPU consumers:
SELECT sql_id, cpu_time/1e6 cpu_s FROM v$sqlstats ORDER BY cpu_time DESC FETCH FIRST 10 ROWS ONLY;
recommendations.impact: Reduces response time and prevents runaway queueing

insights.title: Workload Change: DB Time increased 101%
evidence: Good: DB Time 16067.5 min, Elapsed 49.9 min, AAS 322.3
Bad: DB Time 32276.6 min, Elapsed 109.5 min, AAS 294.7
insights.root_cause: Significant workload increase — more SQL activity, higher concurrency, or data volume growth.
insights.action: Review the SQL and wait event analysis below to identify root cause.

insights.title: SQL Plan Regressions (1 SQLs)
insights.summary: 1 SQL statements got slower per execution while execution count stayed stable. This is a true SQL regression — likely caused by plan flips, stale statistics, or index changes.
insights.root_cause: Execution plan changed due to: stale statistics (run DBMS_STATS), adaptive cursor sharing, index drop/rebuild, or parameter change.
insights.action: Compare execution plans: SELECT * FROM TABLE(DBMS_XPLAN.DISPLAY_AWR(sql_id)). Lock good plans: EXEC DBMS_SPM.LOAD_PLANS_FROM_AWR. Gather fresh stats: EXEC DBMS_STATS.GATHER_TABLE_STATS.

insights.title: 10 New SQL Statements in Bad Period
insights.summary: 10 SQL IDs appeared only in the bad period, consuming 8435s total. These are either new application code, ad-hoc queries, or batch jobs that weren't running before.
evidence: 7zvgkj0z240v4: 948.3s, 37190 execs — INSERT INTO HistWide (DmdUnit, DmdGroup, Loc, Hist
ajjtgm4pjht8g: 451.9s, 15143 execs — DELETE /*OFF NO_INDEX_FFS(h, HISTWIDE_PK) */ /* Hi
5qzq7mrgbbrn5: 3219.0s, 2 execs — MERGE /*+parallel(16)*/ INTO IGPMGR.UDT_POOUT PO U
insights.root_cause: New code deployment, ad-hoc analysis queries, or different batch schedule.
insights.action: Identify source: SELECT MODULE, ACTION FROM V$SQLAREA WHERE SQL_ID IN (...). Check if these need indexes or plan optimization. Verify if these should have been running in this window.

insights.title: 1 New Wait Event Bottlenecks
insights.summary: These wait events appeared only in the bad period — they're entirely new contention points.
insights.root_cause: New contention from new SQL, changed concurrency, or infrastructure issues.
insights.action: Match each new wait event to the SQL or session causing it via ASH data.

insights.title: Incident: Long Running Sql Regression
insights.summary: 10 SQL statement(s) running significantly longer than baseline. Worst: SQL 6dthdax1th2ya — 2207s → 4589s (-5%). Likely plan regression, stale stats, or bind variable skew.
insights.root_cause: 10 SQL statement(s) running significantly longer than baseline. Worst: SQL 6dthdax1th2ya — 2207s → 4589s (-5%). Likely plan regression, stale stats, or bind variable skew.
insights.action: Use Real-Time SQL Monitoring for execution-level visibility. Compare plans: SELECT * FROM TABLE(DBMS_XPLAN.DISPLAY_AWR('<sql_id>')); Gather fresh statistics or use SQL Plan Baseline to lock good plan.

insights.title: ⚠ Baseline Already Stressed — Not a Clean Healthy Reference
insights.summary: The baseline period itself was near-saturated (AAS=322.3 vs 36 CPUs = 895% utilization). The problem period degradation is ON TOP OF an already overloaded system. Performance deltas may understate the true severity.
evidence: Baseline latch hit: 98.0% (degraded — not newly introduced in problem period)
insights.root_cause: The 'good' period was not a clean healthy baseline — it was already at or near capacity. Issues visible in the problem period may have pre-existing roots.
insights.action: Compare against a genuinely idle/healthy period if available. Look at ADDM findings for the baseline period — any findings indicate pre-existing issues. Do not attribute all problem-period issues solely to new workload.

insights.title: Data Volume Increase Detected (7 SQLs)
insights.summary: 7 SQL statements show increased execution counts without per-execution time changes. This indicates data volume growth, not SQL regression. The queries themselves are fine — there's simply more data to process.
evidence: bm5q4vbp3tuvv: execs 282,938 → 514,099 (+82%), per-exec 1.873s → 2.433s (stable) — INSERT INTO histfcst (dmdunit, dmdgroup, loc, mode
c2570kh0gz6sr: execs 6,284 → 9,845 (+57%), per-exec 63.442s → 60.569s (stable) — DELETE /* HistFcstPurgeDAODeleteHint */ /* HistFcs
1d30mpub6tbnu: execs 2,513 → 3,999 (+59%), per-exec 0.785s → 0.911s (stable) — INSERT INTO PROCESSDFU ( PROCESSID, DMDUNIT, DMDGR
insights.root_cause: Data volume increased between the two periods. More rows to process means more executions of the same queries. Check: batch input data size, table row counts, partition growth.
insights.action: Verify data volume change with: SELECT COUNT(*) and partition stats. If volume growth is expected, consider partitioning, parallel DML, or batch scheduling changes. These are NOT SQL tuning candidates — the per-execution time is stable.

insights.title: Wait Profile Shifted — User I/O decreased
insights.summary: The extra DB time is primarily going to User I/O (39% → 34%). I/O increased — data volume change or missing indexes.
insights.root_cause: Wait class 'User I/O' changed by -5.6 percentage points.
insights.action: Cross-reference this with the SQL analysis below to find the specific queries driving this shift.

verdict.root_cause: Average Active Sessions (322.3) exceeds CPU count (36) by 9.0x. DB Time: 16068 min in 50 min elapsed. OS CPU was 28% busy.
verdict.confidence_label: Very high — clear bottleneck, direct evidence
verdict_factors: Primary symptom: 'free buffer waits' at 45.6% DB time (wait class: Configuration)
Top SQL bm5q4vbp3tuvv: 55% DB time, 282,938 execs @ 1.87s/exec
Object under pressure: FCSTDRAFTWIDE () | Guilty SQL: c2570kh0gz6sr

analysis_notes: RULE: ADDM recommendations are guides, not gospel. Validate each recommendation against multiple AWR periods before making system changes. Do NOT increase SGA/PGA based on a single AWR report alone — gather at least 3–5 representative periods.
RULE: AWR averages hide spikes. A 60-minute snapshot can mask a 5-minute crisis. If symptoms are intermittent, split into 15-minute reports or use ASH.
RULE: Buffer Cache Hit Ratio is not a primary health signal. A 99% hit ratio can coexist with a burning system. Investigate wait events and per-execution SQL metrics instead.

investigation_trail.finding: AAS = 322.3 vs 36 CPUs — database severely overloaded, OS CPU busy: 28%
investigation_trail.conclusion: Database is doing 2x more work than CPU capacity. Every other metric matters.

investigation_trail.finding: Snapshot window is 50 minutes — averages hide spikes.
investigation_trail.conclusion: AWR averages can mask a 5-minute crisis inside a 60-minute window. If symptoms are intermittent, split into 15-minute reports (awrrpt.sql) or use ASH (DBA_HIST_ACTIVE_SESS_HISTORY) to isolate the exact problem period.

investigation_trail.finding: #1 non-idle wait: 'free buffer waits' at 45.6% DB time — this is the symptom.
investigation_trail.conclusion: Primary bottleneck category: CONFIGURATION. Next: find Top SQL by Elapsed that links to this wait, then check Segment Statistics to confirm which object is under pressure.

investigation_trail.finding: Top SQL bm5q4vbp3tuvv at 55% DB time — SQL bm5q4vbp3tuvv: 282,938 execs at 1.87s/exec, 4,532 gets/exec.
investigation_trail.conclusion: RULE: Always evaluate per-execution cost, not just totals. High total elapsed from many cheap calls = volume problem. High per-exec cost from few calls = broken query. Check execution plans for full-table scans, missing indexes, and bind variable usage.

investigation_trail.finding: Buffer Hit 92.9%, Soft Parse 99.9%, Library Hit 100.0%, Exec to Parse 86.8%, Latch Hit 98.0%
investigation_trail.conclusion: Efficiency issues detected — see findings.

investigation_trail.finding: Hard Parses: 0/s (ratio 0.2%), Phys Reads: 60051/s, Executes: 459/s, Logons: 1.6/s, Commits: 0/s, Redo: 79.5 MB/s
investigation_trail.conclusion: Load Profile signals detected: Redo=79 MB/s — heavy DML; correlate with log file sync waits; Physical/Logical read ratio=7.8% — buffer cache miss rate is high

investigation_trail.finding: SQL Execute: 962374.1s, PL/SQL: 73.4s, Parse: 5.2s, Hard Parse: 0.9s, Java: 0.0s
investigation_trail.conclusion: Time model distribution looks normal.

investigation_trail.finding: Wait class mix: Configuration=46%, User I/O=39%, Application=9%, CPU=3%, Concurrency=3%
investigation_trail.conclusion: Wait class distribution looks normal — no single class dominates.

investigation_trail.finding: Checked 8 tablespaces. Slow tablespaces: 0
investigation_trail.conclusion: No tablespace IO anomalies detected.

investigation_trail.finding: Built 3 evidence chains linking wait events → segments → SQL
investigation_trail.conclusion: Evidence chains complete.

findings.title: Database Severely Overloaded
findings.detail: Average Active Sessions (322.3) exceeds CPU count (36) by 9.0x. DB Time: 16068 min in 50 min elapsed. OS CPU was 28% busy.

findings.title: Configuration/Resource Sizing Bottleneck
findings.detail: Configuration wait class consuming 45.6% of DB time. Events: free buffer waits. This requires administrative/DDL action, NOT SQL tuning.

findings.title: Single SQL Dominates: 55% of DB Time
findings.detail: SQL bm5q4vbp3tuvv consuming 55% of total DB time. Executions: 282,938, Per-exec: 1.87s, Buffer Gets/Exec: 4,532, Disk Reads/Exec: 95. This SQL IS the bottleneck.

findings.title: db file sequential read: 38.5% DB Time (avg 2.6ms)
findings.detail: Single-block I/O: 38.5% DB time, avg 2.6ms, 140,000,000 waits. Low latency but high volume — the SQL is doing too many index reads.

findings.title: Top 3 SQLs: 98% of DB Time
findings.detail: SQL IDs: bm5q4vbp3tuvv, c2570kh0gz6sr, btwtj4c7adz53. Tuning these 3 statements would address the majority of DB time.

verdict.root_cause: Average Active Sessions (294.7) exceeds CPU count (36) by 8.2x. DB Time: 32277 min in 110 min elapsed. OS CPU was 22% busy.
verdict.confidence_label: Very high — clear bottleneck, direct evidence
verdict_factors: Primary symptom: 'free buffer waits' at 46.8% DB time (wait class: Configuration)
Wait class: Application class=11.3% — row-level locking (enq: TX — row lock contention); blocking sessions
Top SQL bm5q4vbp3tuvv: 65% DB time, 514,099 execs @ 2.43s/exec
Object under pressure: FCSTDRAFTWIDE () | Guilty SQL: c2570kh0gz6sr

analysis_notes: RULE: ADDM recommendations are guides, not gospel. Validate each recommendation against multiple AWR periods before making system changes. Do NOT increase SGA/PGA based on a single AWR report alone — gather at least 3–5 representative periods.
RULE: AWR averages hide spikes. A 60-minute snapshot can mask a 5-minute crisis. If symptoms are intermittent, split into 15-minute reports or use ASH.
RULE: Buffer Cache Hit Ratio is not a primary health signal. A 99% hit ratio can coexist with a burning system. Investigate wait events and per-execution SQL metrics instead.

investigation_trail.finding: AAS = 294.7 vs 36 CPUs — database severely overloaded, OS CPU busy: 22%
investigation_trail.conclusion: Database is doing 2x more work than CPU capacity. Every other metric matters.

investigation_trail.finding: Snapshot window is 110 minutes — averages hide spikes.
investigation_trail.conclusion: AWR averages can mask a 5-minute crisis inside a 60-minute window. If symptoms are intermittent, split into 15-minute reports (awrrpt.sql) or use ASH (DBA_HIST_ACTIVE_SESS_HISTORY) to isolate the exact problem period.

investigation_trail.finding: #1 non-idle wait: 'free buffer waits' at 46.8% DB time — this is the symptom.
investigation_trail.conclusion: Primary bottleneck category: CONFIGURATION. Next: find Top SQL by Elapsed that links to this wait, then check Segment Statistics to confirm which object is under pressure.

investigation_trail.finding: Top SQL bm5q4vbp3tuvv at 65% DB time — SQL bm5q4vbp3tuvv: 514,099 execs at 2.43s/exec, 4,597 gets/exec.
investigation_trail.conclusion: RULE: Always evaluate per-execution cost, not just totals. High total elapsed from many cheap calls = volume problem. High per-exec cost from few calls = broken query. Check execution plans for full-table scans, missing indexes, and bind variable usage.

investigation_trail.finding: Buffer Hit 95.1%, Soft Parse 99.9%, Library Hit 100.0%, Exec to Parse 85.5%, Latch Hit 99.4%
investigation_trail.conclusion: All efficiency metrics within thresholds.

investigation_trail.finding: Hard Parses: 0/s (ratio 0.1%), Phys Reads: 47324/s, Executes: 535/s, Logons: 1.4/s, Commits: 0/s, Redo: 85.0 MB/s
investigation_trail.conclusion: Load Profile signals detected: Redo=85 MB/s — heavy DML; correlate with log file sync waits; Physical/Logical read ratio=5.3% — buffer cache miss rate is high

investigation_trail.finding: SQL Execute: 1930884.6s, PL/SQL: 151.1s, Parse: 16.5s, Hard Parse: 5.8s, Java: 0.0s
investigation_trail.conclusion: Time model distribution looks normal.

investigation_trail.finding: Wait class mix: Configuration=47%, User I/O=34%, Application=11%, Concurrency=5%, CPU=2%
investigation_trail.conclusion: Wait class signals: Application class=11.3% — row-level locking (enq: TX — row lock contention); blocking sessions

investigation_trail.conclusion: No tablespace IO anomalies detected.

86yum5827h64g.fullText: DECLARE c_job_name CONSTANT VARCHAR2(50) :='dmd_upd_fcstnarrow'; v_errcode VARCHAR2(50); v_errdesc VARCHAR2(1000); v_start PLS_INTEGER; v_end PLS_INTEGER; v_min PLS_INTEGER; v_sec PLS_INTEGER; v_volume NUMBER; v_err PLS_INTEGER; v_ohpost DATE; BEGIN v_start := DBMS_UTILITY.GET_TIME; :RC := 0; pkg_log.info('START '||c_job_name); ---Delete override records which are updated in both workbench and worksheet DELETE FROM scpomgr.fcstdraftnarrow t WHERE EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM scpomgr.udt_inhouse_fcst i WHERE record_updated='Y' AND t.dmdunit=i.dmdunit AND t.loc=i.loc AND t.dmdgroup=i.dmdgroup AND t.type=i.type AND t.startdate=i.startdate AND t.model=i.model); pkg_log.info('Records deleted from fcstdraftnarrow for INHOUSE Overrides: '||SQL%ROWCOUNT); COMMIT; ---Delete override records which are updated in both workbench and worksheet DELETE FROM scpomgr.fcstdraftnarrow t WHERE EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM scpomgr.udt_by_fcst i WHERE record_updated='Y' AND t.dmdunit=i.dmdunit AND t.loc=i.loc AND t.dmdgroup=i.dmdgroup AND t.type=i.type AND t.startdate=i.startdate AND t.model=i.model); pkg_log.info('Records deleted from fcstdraftnarrow for BY Overrides: '||SQL%ROWCOUNT); COMMIT; MERGE INTO scpomgr.fcstdraftnarrow f USING (SELECT /*+ PARALLEL(8) */ dmdunit, dmdgroup, loc, startdate, dur, type, nvl(TRIM(fcstid), 'OVERRIDE360') fcstid, qty, model from scpomgr.udt_inhouse_fcst WHERE record_updated='Y') s ON(f.dmdunit = s.dmdunit AND f.loc = s.loc AND f.dmdgroup = s.dmdgroup AND f.startDate=s.startDate AND f.type=s.type AND f.fcstid=s.fcstid AND f.model=s.model) WHEN NOT MATCHED THEN INSERT(f.dmdunit, f.dmdgroup, f.loc, f.startdate, f.dur, f.type, f.fcstid, f.qty, f.model ) VALUES(s.dmdunit, s.dmdgroup, s.loc, s.startdate, s.dur, s.type, s.fcstid, s.qty, s.model ); pkg_log.info('Records inserted from UDT_INHOUSE_FCST to fcstdraftnarrow: '||SQL%ROWCOUNT); COMMIT; MERGE INTO scpomgr.fcstdraftnarrow f USING (SELECT /*+ PARALLEL(8) */ dmdunit, dmdgroup, loc, startdate, dur, type, nvl(TRIM(fcstid), 'OVERRIDE360') fcstid, qty, model from scpomgr.udt_by_fcst ab WHERE record_updated='Y' and exists (select 1 from SCPOMGR.DFU abc where ab.dmdunit=abc.dmdunit and ab.dmdgroup=abc.dmdgroup and ab.loc=abc.loc and ab.model=abc.model)) s ON(f.dmdunit = s.dmdunit AND f.loc = s.loc AND f.dmdgroup = s.dmdgroup AND f.startDate=s.startDate AND f.type=s.type AND f.fcstid=s.fcstid AND f.model=s.model) WHEN NOT MATCHED THEN INSERT(f.dmdunit, f.dmdgroup, f.loc, f.startdate, f.dur, f.type, f.fcstid, f.qty, f.model ) VALUES( s.dmdunit, s.dmdgroup, s.loc, s.startdate, s.dur, s.type, s.fcstid, s.qty, s.model ); pkg_log.info('Records inserted from UDT_BY_FCST to fcstdraftnarrow: '||SQL%ROWCOUNT); COMMIT; v_end := DBMS_UTILITY.GET_TIME; v_min := FLOOR(((v_end - v_start) / 100) / 60); v_sec := MOD(((v_end - v_start) / 100), 60); pkg_log.info('TIME TAKEN to update fcstdraftnarrow:'||v_min||' MINS '||v_sec||' SECS'); EXCEPTION WHEN OTHERS THEN pkg_job.fatal_error; :RC := '1'; ROLLBACK; v_errdesc := SUBSTR(SQLERRM, 1, 1000); v_errcode := TO_CHAR(SQLCODE); pkg_log.error(c_job_name||' ORACLE ERROR -'||v_errcode||' '||v_errdesc); END;

c7f38s4dd5u7r.fullText: select /*+rule */ A.sql_id, A.sql_fulltext from ( select rownum rwnm, sql_id, sql_fulltext from v$sql where sql_id in (select sql_id from v$session a, v$process b where a.paddr= b.addr and b.spid in (6165, 6177, 6169, 6173, 3782, 10184, 9292, 9510, 9552, 9604, 9646, 9737) )) A, (select min(rownum) mrn, sql_id from v$sql where sql_id in (select sql_id from v$session a, v$process b where a.paddr= b.addr and b.spid in (6165, 6177, 6169, 6173, 3782, 10184, 9292, 9510, 9552, 9604, 9646, 9737) ) group by sql_id) B where A.sql_id=B.sql_id and A.rwnm=b.mrn

dtknc5b5g6h0h.fullText: UPDATE SRE_NODE SET LOCKERS_NODE_ID = :B1 WHERE (((NODE_STATE = :B5 ) AND (SRE_NODE.STOP_REQUEST_DATE < (SYS_EXTRACT_UTC(SYSTIMESTAMP) - :B4 )) ) OR (NODE_STATE = :B3 ) ) AND (CURRENT_JOB_ID = :B2 AND LOCKERS_NODE_ID IS NULL AND NODE_ID <> :B1 AND ROWNUM = 1) RETURNING NODE_ID INTO :O0

fp77pwh3acs0v.fullText: MERGE INTO SCPOMGR.FCSTDRAFTNARROW F USING (SELECT /*+ PARALLEL(8) */ DMDUNIT, DMDGROUP, LOC, STARTDATE, DUR, TYPE, NVL(TRIM(FCSTID), 'OVERRIDE360') FCSTID, QTY, MODEL FROM SCPOMGR.UDT_INHOUSE_FCST WHERE RECORD_UPDATED='Y') S ON(F.DMDUNIT = S.DMDUNIT AND F.LOC = S.LOC AND F.DMDGROUP = S.DMDGROUP AND F.STARTDATE=S.STARTDATE AND F.TYPE=S.TYPE AND F.FCSTID=S.FCSTID AND F.MODEL=S.MODEL) WHEN NOT MATCHED THEN INSERT(F.DMDUNIT, F.DMDGROUP, F.LOC, F.STARTDATE, F.DUR, F.TYPE, F.FCSTID, F.QTY, F.MODEL ) VALUES(S.DMDUNIT, S.DMDGROUP, S.LOC, S.STARTDATE, S.DUR, S.TYPE, S.FCSTID, S.QTY, S.MODEL )

_sql_text_map.1d30mpub6tbnu: INSERT INTO PROCESSDFU ( PROCESSID, DMDUNIT, DMDGROUP, LOC, MODEL, BATCHNUM, DMDCAL, ADJ_DMDPOSTDATE, FCSTHOR, PICKBESTSW, ADJ_HISTSTARTDATE, STAGE, PROCERR ) VALUES ( :B12 , :B1 , :B2 , :B3 , :B4 , :B5 , :B6 , :B7 , :B8 , :B9 , :B10 , 0, :B11 )
_sql_text_map.1shhygvj3zw36: SELECT M.APPLICATION_NAME, M.MEASURE_ID FROM MD_MEASURE_APPLICATION M WHERE M.MEASURE_ID = :1
_sql_text_map.2qjq3d3u54ajq: SELECT 1 FROM GV$SESSION A WHERE SID = :B3 AND SERIAL# = :B2 AND INST_ID = :B1 AND EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM MANUGISTICS_SCHEMA B WHERE A.USERNAME = B.SCHEMA_NAME AND A.USERNAME <> 'SYS' AND A.USERNAME <> 'SYSTEM' AND A.USERNAME <> 'JDA_SYSTEM' AND A.USERNAME IS NOT NULL)
_sql_text_map.4qguf81v4r5mx: UPDATE SRE_NODE SET LOCKERS_NODE_ID = :B4 , STOP_REQUEST_DATE = SYS_EXTRACT_UTC(SYSTIMESTAMP), NODE_STATE = :B3 WHERE NODE_STATE = :B2 AND ROWNUM = 1 AND SRE_NODE.CURRENT_JOB_ID = :B1 RETURNING NODE_ID INTO :O0
_sql_text_map.4tf42bahkszhd: UPDATE SRE_ACTION SET LOCKERS_NODE_ID = :B4 , LAST_UPDATE_DATE = SYS_EXTRACT_UTC (SYSTIMESTAMP) WHERE LOCKERS_NODE_ID IS NULL AND ACTION_GROUP_SET_ID = :B3 AND ROWNUM = 1 AND STATE <> :B2 AND GROUP_NUMBER = :B1 RETURNING ACTION_NUMBER, GROUP_NUMBER, NAME, STATE, RETRY_COUNT INTO :O0 , :O1 , :O2 , :O3 , :O4
_sql_text_map.5dqz0hqtp9fru: select /*+ connect_by_filtering index(sysauth$ i_sysauth1) */ privilege#, bitand(nvl(option$, 0), 72), grantee#, level from sysauth$ connect by grantee#=prior privilege# and privilege#>0 start with grantee#=:1 and privilege#>0
_sql_text_map.5uvns4qk0vu5w: SELECT COUNT(*) FROM SRE_NODE WHERE SRE_NODE.NODE_STATE <> :B3 AND SRE_NODE.NODE_STATE <> :B2 AND SRE_NODE.CURRENT_JOB_ID = :B1
_sql_text_map.7f9g8as2nwyfr: UPDATE /*+ PARALLEL(32) */ SCPOMGR.DFU SET U_CALCMODEL=1 WHERE MODEL IN('POS_AVS', 'POS_LEW')
_sql_text_map.85ws2usb2z1t2: UPDATE /*+ parallel */ SCPOMGR.AVSGRAVESPARAM SET NPITMAX = 1 WHERE NPITMAX > 1
_sql_text_map.865qwpcdyggkk: select spare6 from user$ where user#=:1
_sql_text_map.86yum5827h64g: DECLARE c_job_name CONSTANT VARCHAR2(50) :='dmd_upd_fcstnarrow'; v_errcode VARCHAR2(50); v_errdesc VARCHAR2(1000); v_start PLS_INTEGER; v_end PLS_INTEGER; v_min PLS_INTEGER; v_sec PLS_INTEGER; v_volume NUMBER; v_err PLS_INTEGER; v_ohpost DATE; BEGIN v_start := DBMS_UTILITY.GET_TIME; :RC := 0; pkg_log.info('START '||c_job_name); ---Delete override records which are updated in both workbench and worksheet DELETE FROM scpomgr.fcstdraftnarrow t WHERE EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM scpomgr.udt_inhouse_fcst i WHERE record_updated='Y' AND t.dmdunit=i.dmdunit AND t.loc=i.loc AND t.dmdgroup=i.dmdgroup AND t.type=i.type AND t.startdate=i.startdate AND t.model=i.model); pkg_log.info('Records deleted from fcstdraftnarrow for INHOUSE Overrides: '||SQL%ROWCOUNT); COMMIT; ---Delete override records which are updated in both workbench and worksheet DELETE FROM scpomgr.fcstdraftnarrow t WHERE EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM scpomgr.udt_by_fcst i WHERE record_updated='Y' AND t.dmdunit=i.dmdunit AND t.loc=i.loc AND t.dmdgroup=i.dmdgroup AND t.type=i.type AND t.startdate=i.startdate AND t.model=i.model); pkg_log.info('Records deleted from fcstdraftnarrow for BY Overrides: '||SQL%ROWCOUNT); COMMIT; MERGE INTO scpomgr.fcstdraftnarrow f USING (SELECT /*+ PARALLEL(8) */ dmdunit, dmdgroup, loc, startdate, dur, type, nvl(TRIM(fcstid), 'OVERRIDE360') fcstid, qty, model from scpomgr.udt_inhouse_fcst WHERE record_updated='Y') s ON(f.dmdunit = s.dmdunit AND f.loc = s.loc AND f.dmdgroup = s.dmdgroup AND f.startDate=s.startDate AND f.type=s.type AND f.fcstid=s.fcstid AND f.model=s.model) WHEN NOT MATCHED THEN INSERT(f.dmdunit, f.dmdgroup, f.loc, f.startdate, f.dur, f.type, f.fcstid, f.qty, f.model ) VALUES(s.dmdunit, s.dmdgroup, s.loc, s.startdate, s.dur, s.type, s.fcstid, s.qty, s.model ); _sql_text_map.8hqpjjsuv1vrb: SELECT c.schema_name, c.table_name, c.column_name, position FROM all_constraints uc, all_cons_columns ucc, md_column_info c, md_table_info t WHERE uc.status='ENABLED' AND c.is_configured=1 AND t.is_configured=1 AND uc.constraint_type='P' AND uc.constraint_name=ucc.constraint_name AND c.schema_name=t.schema_name AND c.schema_name=uc.owner AND c.schema_name=ucc.owner AND c.table_name=uc.table_name AND c.table_name=ucc.table_name AND c.table_name=t.table_name AND c.column_name = ucc.column_name ORDER BY uc.constraint_name
_sql_text_map.8uv0zhc7bk7ug: SELECT /*+ PARALLEL(48) */ /* ProcessDFUDAOHintBatch */ /* ProcessDFUDAOHintBatch*/ d.dmdunit, d.dmdgroup, d.loc, d.model, d.dmdcal, trunc(d.dmdpostdate) adj_dmdpostdate, d.pickbestsw, d.fcsthor fcsthor, d.modeldate adj_histstartdate FROM dfu d WHERE (d.dmdunit, d.dmdgroup, d.loc, d.model) IN ( SELECT SCPOMGR.DFU.DMDUNIT, SCPOMGR.DFU.DMDGROUP, SCPOMGR.DFU.LOC, SCPOMGR.DFU.MODEL FROM SCPOMGR.DFU, SCPOMGR.DFUVIEW RELX_DFUVIEW where (RELX_DFUVIEW.U_DFU_LEVEL = '132' OR RELX_DFUVIEW.U_DFU_LEVEL = '133') and SCPOMGR.DFU.DMDUNIT=RELX_DFUVIEW.DMDUNIT AND SCPOMGR.DFU.DMDGROUP=RELX_DFUVIEW.DMDGROUP AND SCPOMGR.DFU.LOC=RELX_DFUVIEW.LOC) ORDER BY d.dmdcal, d.dmdunit, d.dmdgroup, d.loc, d.model

_sql_text_map.9su77bm5zw0gw: SELECT /* ForecastRetrievalDaoWithEventsSelectHintForFcstDraftNarrow */ f.dmdunit, f.dmdgroup, f.loc, f.model, f.startdate, f.dur, f.fcstid, f.type, f.qty FROM ProcessDFU pdfu, FcstDraftNarrow f WHERE pdfu.processid = :1 AND pdfu.stage = 0 AND pdfu.batchnum = :2 AND pdfu.dmdunit = f.dmdunit AND pdfu.dmdgroup = f.dmdgroup AND pdfu.loc = f.loc AND pdfu.model = f.model AND f.type in ( 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 9 )

_sql_text_map.ajjtgm4pjht8g: DELETE /*OFF NO_INDEX_FFS(h, HISTWIDE_PK) */ /* HistPurgeDAOWideDeleteHint */ /* HistPurgeDAOWideDeleteHint*/ FROM histwide hw WHERE (hw.dmdunit, hw.dmdgroup, hw.loc, hw.histstream, hw.type) IN (SELECT /*OFF USE_NL(PR DM H) */ /* HistPurgeDAOWideSelectHint --Need more tuning in PT. */ /* HistPurgeDAOWideSelectHint */ /* HistPurgeDAOWideSelectHint*/ dm.todmdunit, dm.todmdgroup, dm.todfuloc, m.histstream, h.type FROM dfumap dm, processreconcile pr, model m, histwide h WHERE pr.map = :1 AND pr.action_number = :2 AND pr.action_group = :3 AND pr.action_group_set_id = :4 AND pr.map = dm.map AND pr.dmdunit = dm.todmdunit AND pr.dmdgroup = dm.todmdgroup AND pr.loc = dm.todfuloc AND pr.model = dm.tomodel AND dm.tomodel = m.model AND dm.todmdunit = h.dmdunit AND dm.todmdgroup = h.dmdgroup AND dm.todfuloc = h.loc AN D ((dm.histtype=1 AND h.type<>3) OR (dm.histtype=2 AND h.type=1) OR (dm.histtype=3 AND h.type=2)))
_sql_text_map.b61xcu0nt77xk: SELECT /* OBDestSKUWorkCalHint */ cal.cal as cal, cal.type as type, cal.master as master, cal.numfcstper as numfcstper, caldata.eff as eff, caldata.opt as opt, caldata.repeat as repeat, caldata.avail as avail, caldata.altcal as altcal, caldata.perwgt as perwgt, caldata.allocwgt as allocwgt, caldata.covdur as covdur FROM CAL cal, CALDATA caldata WHERE cal.cal in (select /* OBDestSKUWorkCalSubQHint1 */ workingcal from skuplanningparam sp WHERE (item, loc) IN (SELECT /* OBDestSKUWorkCalSubQHint2 */ item, dest FROM sourcing s WHERE source = :1 and dest = :2 )) AND cal.type = :3 AND cal.cal = caldata.cal ORDER BY cal.cal, caldata.eff
